name: Capricorn
description: Capricorn, also known as the Goat-Fish, is a prominent figure in Greek mythology, often associated with the zodiac sign of the same name. Depicted as a hybrid creature with the upper body of a goat and the tail of a fish, Capricorn's origins are linked to the god Pan, who, while fleeing the monster Typhon, transformed into a fish to escape. However, the transformation was incomplete, resulting in the goat-fish form. Capricorn is also associated with the nurturing Amalthea, the goat who suckled Zeus in his infancy, further solidifying its connection to provision and care. This duality reflects Capricorn's complex symbolism, embodying both terrestrial and aquatic qualities, representing ambition, perseverance, resourcefulness, and adaptability. The creature is often seen as a symbol of winter solstice, marking the sun's return and the promise of renewed life. Its presence in the zodiac highlights its importance in astrological beliefs and its enduring legacy in Western culture.
subtitle: The Goat-Fish of Greek Mythology
appearance: Capricorn is typically represented as a hybrid creature with the forequarters of a goat, including horns and hooves, and the hindquarters of a fish, complete with a scaled tail. The goat portion is usually depicted as strong and muscular, while the fish tail is often ornate and flowing. Colors can vary, but depictions often include earthy tones for the goat half and blues or greens for the fish tail.
habitat: Capricorn's mythological habitat is varied, reflecting its dual nature. As a creature linked to Pan, it is associated with mountainous regions and wild landscapes. However, its fish tail also connects it to aquatic environments, such as rivers, lakes, and the sea. In astrological contexts, it is linked to the celestial sphere and the zodiac.
behavior: Capricorn's behavior is often interpreted through its symbolic meaning. As a representation of ambition and perseverance, it embodies a determined and goal-oriented nature. Its association with both land and water suggests adaptability and resourcefulness. In some interpretations, it is also seen as a protector or guardian, reflecting its connection to Amalthea and the nurturing of Zeus.

keyMyths.title: Pan and Typhon
keyMyths.description: According to one myth, Pan, while attempting to escape the monstrous Typhon, jumped into the Nile River. In his panic, he only managed to transform his lower half into that of a fish, resulting in the hybrid form of Capricorn. This story explains the creature's dual nature and its association with both land and water.

keyMyths.title: Amalthea and Zeus
keyMyths.description: Amalthea, the goat who nursed Zeus in his infancy, is sometimes linked to Capricorn. After Zeus became the ruler of the gods, he honored Amalthea by placing her image among the stars as the constellation Capricorn. This connection highlights Capricorn's association with nurturing and provision.

keyMyths.title: Capricorn as a Zodiac Sign
keyMyths.description: In astrology, Capricorn is one of the twelve zodiac signs, associated with individuals born between December 22 and January 19. Astrologers attribute traits such as ambition, discipline, and responsibility to those born under this sign, reflecting the symbolic characteristics of the mythological Capricorn.

sources.author: Hyginus
sources.description: A collection of mythological stories, including the tale of Pan's transformation into Capricorn while fleeing Typhon.

sources.description: A comprehensive account of Greek mythology, detailing the story of Amalthea and her role in nurturing Zeus.
